title: Design a Trending Hashtags System
shortDescription: Design a highly scalable system that tracks and computes the top K trending hashtags (e.g., top 30-50) across different time windows (last 5/15/30/60 minutes or custom intervals) for billions of users, with support for filtering by geography (local/global) and categories (food, sports, politics).

Were You Asked This?
Design a Trending Hashtags System

Design a highly scalable system that tracks and computes the top K trending hashtags (e.g., top 30-50) across different time windows (last 5/15/30/60 minutes or custom intervals) for billions of users, with support for filtering by geography (local/global) and categories (food, sports, politics).

Asked at:

Google

Meta

Atlassian

LinkedIn

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Trending Hashtags is a real-time analytics feature built into social platforms that surfaces the most popular hashtags over recent time windows, like "last 1/5/15/60 minutes" or the past hour. Think of Twitter Trends or Instagram Explore showing the top topics globally, by country, or within categories like sports and politics.

Interviewers ask this because it exercises your ability to design high-throughput streaming systems, compute top-K over sliding windows, and handle skewed traffic (hot hashtags) at global scale. It probes your understanding of event-time processing, approximate algorithms, partitioning to avoid hotspots, fault tolerance, and low-latency serving. Expect to balance accuracy vs freshness vs cost while supporting filters (geo/category) and user-specified windows.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
YouTube Top K

System design answer key for designing a feature to find the most popular videos like YouTube's trending section, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to view the top K (e.g., 30–50) hashtags for a chosen time window (e.g., last 1/5/15/60 minutes or up to 12 hours).

Users should be able to filter trending hashtags by geography (global, country, city) and by category (e.g., food, sports, politics).

Users should be able to specify a custom time interval within allowed bounds and receive a ranked list quickly.

Users should experience near real-time freshness of trends, with changes reflected within a target latency (e.g., a few seconds).

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you implement sliding and custom time windows while handling out-of-order and late events at scale?

How do you deal with hot hashtags and avoid contention while sustaining massive write throughput?

How will you compute and serve top-K per geography and category with low latency and manageable cost?

What correctness and fault-tolerance guarantees will you provide end-to-end (ingestion, processing, serving)?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Writes

Billions of users and 100M+ daily posts create a write-heavy stream of hashtag events. You must partition ingestion, pre-aggregate, and batch to sustain throughput without overwhelming downstream systems.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Trending topics create extreme skew where a few hashtags receive the majority of updates. Avoid hot shards and locks using virtual sharding, local aggregation, and heavy-hitter algorithms.

Read More
Real-time Updates

The product’s value is freshness. You need a pipeline that ingests, aggregates, and updates top-K leaderboards in seconds, with push/pull mechanisms to reflect changes quickly.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides durable, high-throughput ingestion with partitioning by key (e.g., hashtag or geo) and replay for recovery. It decouples producers from processors and supports exactly-once semantics with transactional writes.

Read More
Flink

Flink excels at event-time windowing, watermarks, stateful streaming, and incremental aggregations. It supports sliding/tumbling windows, late data handling, and exactly-once state via checkpoints—ideal for trending computations.

Read More
Redis

Redis offers in-memory, low-latency serving of leaderboards using sorted sets and hash structures. It’s well-suited for caching top-K per geo/category/window with TTLs and fast updates from stream processors.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

YouTube Top K

Both require maintaining and serving top-K items with frequent updates, handling skewed popularity, and balancing precomputation with cache-based serving.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

Counting clicks per ad over time windows mirrors counting hashtag usages; both rely on streaming ingestion, windowed aggregations, and handling late events.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Real-time updates, high fan-in writes, and the need for low-latency read paths are shared concerns; techniques for hot key mitigation and push updates carry over.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Using a single global aggregator or lock for counting hashtags

This creates a hot shard and single point of failure, collapsing throughput under skew. It prevents horizontal scaling and leads to high tail latencies when a topic explodes.

Insisting on exact global counts and strict consistency for all windows

Exactness across sliding windows, dimensions, and late events dramatically increases complexity and latency. It sacrifices availability and cost without improving user value; prefer bounded-lateness and eventual consistency.

Computing trends on read by scanning raw logs

Query-time aggregation over massive logs yields seconds-to-minutes latencies and unpredictable costs. You need streaming pre-aggregation and cached top-K per window/dimension to meet real-time SLAs.

M
ManagingBlushPanther966
Premium
• 4个月前

One key difference not quite shown in all the solutions and is even missing in the first requirement is that this is a trending system. You can have a hashtag that is in the Top-K that is not trending. #Love may be mentioned millions of times an hour, but if #earthquake goes from 100s to 10K then that is a trend that should pop up. The solutions need to account for calculating rate of change as the data comes in.

2

Reply
P
PreviousBlueHawk549
Premium
• 3个月前

isn't it the top K in a given time window. If interaction rate if certain hashtags is higher than others, it will have higher count in that time window and should find place in top K.
